title: Genesis 25
subtitle: Abraham's Death; the Birth of Jacob and Esau

poi.name: Cave of Machpelah
poi.text: Isaac and Ishmael bury Abraham together at Machpelah — the first act of the two divided sons after their separation. The shared burial act in the family tomb is the geography of reconciliation: together at the covenant's designated resting place.

notes.note: MacArthur: Abraham died at 175 years old, 'a good old age, an old man and full of years.' The Hebrew 'full of years' (sava yamim) means satisfied with life -- a life that reached its purpose. The covenant promises were not fully possessed in his lifetime (Heb 11:13) but he died in faith, seeing them from a distance.

MacArthur: Isaac and Ishmael buried their father together at Machpelah. The two sons who represent the covenant division -- one chosen, one not -- are united at their father's grave. The burial of Abraham reconciles what the covenant had divided. It anticipates the final reconciliation when all things are gathered under Christ (Eph 1:10).

heb.gloss: to expire / to breathe one's last
heb.paragraph: Abraham "breathed his last (yiggawaʿ) and died at a good old age, an old man and full of years" (v.8). The verb gāwaʿ ("to expire") is more solemn than the ordinary word for death (mût) — it describes the cessation of vital breath. The phrase "full of years" (śābēaʿ yāmîm — satisfied/sated with days) is a covenantal evaluation: Abraham lived out the full measure of his days in covenant faithfulness. The same phrase describes Isaac (35:29), Job (42:17), and David (1 Chr 29:28).

Abraham's burial in the cave of Machpelah (v.9-10) — by both Isaac and Ishmael together — is the first time the two half-brothers are presented together in the narrative after the expulsion of Hagar (21:14). The shared burial of their father is a moment of covenant reconciliation — the two streams of Abraham's descendants uniting to honour their common ancestor in the covenant ground of Machpelah. The grave is the one place where the divided family temporarily becomes one.

"After Abraham's death, God blessed his son Isaac" (v.11). The covenant blessing transfers from generation to generation — Abraham's death does not interrupt the covenant promise but releases it to the next recipient. Isaac lives near Beer Lahai Roi — the well associated with Hagar and Ishmael (16:14) — a geographical detail that keeps the two covenant streams in view simultaneously even as the narrative focus shifts to the covenant line through Isaac.

panels.ctx: Abraham died "in a good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people" — one of the most gracious death notices in Scripture. The phrase "gathered to his people" implies belief in an afterlife beyond the grave, a gathering of the dead. Most significantly: Isaac and Ishmael bury their father together. After years of separation, the two sons are reunited in grief and honor. It is the last recorded moment of reconciliation in the Abraham story.

cross.note: Jacob also "gathered to his people" and is buried in Machpelah — the patriarchal death formula repeating.
Abraham "died in faith, not having received the things promised" — the seemingly incomplete life is actually a life of faith completed, awaiting future fulfillment.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abraham marries Keturah after Sarah's death and has six more sons. Sarna: "The six sons of Keturah are the ancestors of various Arabian tribes. Their inclusion in Abraham's genealogy extends the scope of the Abrahamic blessing beyond Isaac and Ishmael to a third line of descendants. Yet the covenant heir is clear: Abraham gave gifts to the other sons but 'gave everything he owned to Isaac.'

Abraham dies at 175 and is buried by Isaac and Ishmael together. Sarna: "The two brothers burying their father together is the narrative reconciliation of the Ishmael expulsion. The estranged half-brothers reunite for the patriarch's burial — the same pattern will appear with Jacob's sons burying him (50:12-13). Family divisions are transcended at the grave."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abraham died "at a good old age, an old man and full of years." Alter: "The death notice is one of the most peaceful in the Bible — three qualifications of completeness: good age, old, full. Abraham's life has been the story of promise, testing, and faith. The conclusion is not triumph or drama but the quiet fullness of a life completely lived. He is 'gathered to his people' — joined to his ancestors in Sheol."

Isaac and Ishmael burying their father together is one of Genesis's most moving scenes. Alter notes the narrative economy: no dialogue, no explanation of how they came to meet. The silence speaks: whatever divided them in life, Abraham's death is answered by his sons' shared act of honour.

Calvin on Abraham's "good old age": "Moses here commends the happiness of the death of the holy patriarch. He lived to a good age, was full of years, and died — as an athlete who has run his race and reaches the finishing post in contentment. The blessedness of Abraham's death is the seal on a life of faith." Death as completion, not defeat.

Calvin: Abraham had lived long enough since he had fulfilled his course — seen Isaac married, seen his grandsons. God graciously allowed him to depart with his work accomplished and his faith vindicated.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "gathered to his people" (ne'esaf el ammav) explains this Hebrew idiom for death as distinct from burial — it refers to joining the dead ancestors in Sheol, not merely to the physical burial. The note observes this is one of the Bible's most direct references to the afterlife as a gathering of the dead, predating the explicit resurrection theology of Daniel and the NT.

The NET note on Machpelah traces the cave's significance: purchased in ch.23 for Sarah, containing Abraham, later Isaac and Rebekah, Leah, and Jacob (49:29-32). Machpelah becomes the patriarchal family tomb — the one piece of covenant land actually possessed in the generation of promise.

poi.name: From Havilah to Shur
poi.text: Ishmael's twelve sons settle from Havilah (eastern Arabia) to Shur (the Egyptian border) "in the vicinity of all his brothers." The territory is vast — the entire Arabian peninsula and Sinai. The covenant's outside has its own geography of fulfilment.

notes.note: MacArthur: The genealogy of Ishmael (the twelve princes) fulfils the promise of Genesis 17:20. God's promise to Ishmael is kept with the same precision as his promises to Isaac. The distinction between covenant election (Isaac's line) and general providential blessing (Ishmael's line) does not diminish the reality of God's care for Ishmael's descendants.

MacArthur: 'They settled in the area from Havilah to Shur, near the eastern border of Egypt, as you go toward Ashur. And they lived in hostility toward all the tribes related to them' -- the settlement and the conflict both fulfil the angel's prophecy in 16:12. Ishmael's descendants become the peoples of the Arabian peninsula and the historical context for Israel's ongoing conflicts.

heb.paragraph: The twelve princes of Ishmael (vv.13-16) fulfil the promise of 17:20: "I will make him into a great nation." The twelve-tribe structure of Ishmael's descendants mirrors the twelve tribes of Israel — a structural parallel suggesting that God's covenant purposes for Abraham's entire lineage have a symmetry: both Ishmael and Jacob/Israel generate twelve-tribe confederacies. The fulfilment of the Ishmael promise occurs within the text that is transitioning to the Jacob narrative — a reminder that God's faithfulness is not narrowed to one covenant line.

heb.gloss: to fall / to settle / to camp
heb.paragraph: "And they lived in hostility toward all the tribes related to them" (v.18) — or more literally, "he settled (nāpal) to the east of all his relatives." The word nāpal ("fell upon/settled") in territorial contexts means to settle in a region — Ishmael's descendants occupy the territory from Egypt to Assyria (v.18). The "hostility" (literally "before the face of") translates the same phrase used of Ishmael in 16:12 — a perpetual orientation of independence and resistance that has characterised his descendants throughout history.

"This is the account of the family line of Abraham's son Ishmael" (v.12) — the tôledôt formula for Ishmael is brief (vv.12-18) before the narrative transitions to "the account of the family line of Abraham's son Isaac" (v.19). The structural contrast is deliberate: Ishmael's tôledôt is quickly summarised; Isaac's (the covenant line) will unfold over the next thirty-five chapters. The brevity is not theological dismissal but narrative focus — the covenant story must be told fully; the peripheral story is acknowledged and closed.

panels.ctx: The oracle to Rebekah (v.23) overturns the natural birth order before the boys are born — "the older shall serve the younger." The entire Jacob story unfolds under this reversal. The birthright sale (vv.29–34) shows Jacob acquiring by cunning what God had already promised by oracle — creating a character study of someone who grasps at what God would have given anyway. The pattern will cost Jacob decades of exile.

cross.note: Paul uses the Esau/Jacob reversal as his foundational text for divine election: "Though they were not yet born and had done nothing either good or bad...she was told, 'The older will serve the younger.'" God's choice precedes human action.
Hebrews uses Esau as a warning against being "sexually immoral or unholy like Esau, who sold his birthright for a single meal" — a type of those who trade the spiritual for the immediate.
"Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated" — the Malachi reference cited by Paul in Romans 9 takes the Genesis narrative into prophetic and theological territory.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The toledoth of Ishmael closes the Ishmael narrative before the Jacob story begins. Sarna: "The twelve sons of Ishmael mirror the twelve sons of Jacob — the parallel genealogies suggest that both brothers are genuinely blessed, though in different ways. The twelve princes of Ishmael fulfil the promise of Genesis 17:20. The Ishmaelite tribes occupied the Arabian desert and the Sinai — the regions bordering Israel throughout her history."

The Ishmael genealogy closes with the note that Ishmael's descendants 'lived in hostility toward all their brothers.' Sarna notes this confirms the divine promise to Hagar (16:11-12) as historical pattern. The promise to the covenant's margin also holds.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ishmael dies at 137, and "they lived in hostility toward all the tribes related to them." Alter: "The closing note on the Ishmaelites echoes the birth oracle of 16:12 — the wild donkey of a man, hand against everyone. Ishmael's story ends as it was predicted to begin: with his descendants at conflict with their neighbours. The prophecy was character description, not just curse."

The twelve princes of Ishmael parallel the twelve tribes of Israel. Alter notes the symmetry is deliberate: the same God who multiplied Jacob's seed multiplied Ishmael's. The 'father of many nations' promise (17:5) encompasses both lines.

The listing of Ishmael's descendants shows that God's common grace keeps its word. Calvin: "Although Ishmael was not the heir of the promise, yet God performed what he had promised concerning him. The twelve princes are the fulfilment of the promise of Genesis 17:20. God does not lie, even to those outside the covenant."

Calvin: If God maintains the word given to the outsider, how much more certainly will he maintain the covenant given to the heir. The covenant people should be encouraged by God's faithfulness to his lesser promises.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Ishmael's twelve princes observes that the parallel with Jacob's twelve sons is structurally significant — both patriarchal lines produce twelve tribal leaders. The note identifies the Ishmaelite clans with various Arabian tribes known from ancient Near Eastern sources.

The NET note on the Ishmael genealogy observes it is a closing bracket on the Abrahamic narrative. As the narrative shifted from Terah to Abraham in 11:27, it now shifts from Abraham to Isaac. The Ishmael and Isaac genealogies mark the narrative transition.

sections.header: Verses 19–34 — The Birth of Jacob and Esau; the Birthright Sold

tl.name: Jacob and Esau born (Isaac age 60) — this passage
tl.text: Twenty years of barrenness before Jacob and Esau are born. The covenant's births are always delayed beyond natural expectation. Jacob grasps Esau's heel at birth.

notes.note: MacArthur: Isaac prayed for his barren wife (v.21) -- 25 years after God had promised the covenant line through Isaac, the barrenness continues. The pattern of barrenness followed by miraculous conception recurs: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel. Each birth is a demonstration that the covenant continues by divine provision, not human fertility.

MacArthur: Esau's sale of his birthright illustrates the spiritual principle that the person who lives for immediate gratification despises eternal inheritance. 'He ate and drank, and then got up and left. So Esau despised his birthright' (v.34) -- the narrative's verdict is theological, not just biographical. The writer of Hebrews calls Esau 'godless' (Heb 12:16) for this reason.

heb.paragraph: Rebekah is ʿăqārāh — barren (v.21). The pattern is now established: each covenant woman in Genesis is barren until God opens her womb (Sarah: ch.21; Rebekah: ch.25; Rachel: ch.30). The barrenness is not accidental but theological — it creates the condition in which the divine gift character of the covenant child is unmistakable. Each birth is a demonstration of divine power over the biological impossibility that would otherwise close the covenant line. Paul reads this pattern in Gal 4:23-27: "the son of the free woman was born through the promise."

heb.gloss: to seek / to enquire / to resort to
heb.paragraph: Isaac "prayed (wayyeʿtar) to the LORD on behalf of his wife" and Rebekah "went to enquire (lidrōš) of the LORD" (vv.21-22). Two verbs of seeking God: ʿātar ("to pray urgently/to burn incense before") and dāraš ("to seek/enquire" — the technical term for consulting an oracle or prophet). The covenant couple's response to barrenness is to pray and seek divine revelation — the model for all covenant problem-solving. Prayer precedes and accompanies the promise's fulfilment.

"The older (rab) will serve the younger (ṣāʿîr)" (v.23) — the divine oracle overturns the natural order of primogeniture before the twins are born. The bĕkōr (firstborn) convention — Ishmael, Esau, and Reuben all forfeited or were bypassed in their firstborn status — runs through Genesis as a theological statement: covenant election is not natural hierarchy but divine choice. Paul reads the oracle in Rom 9:10-13: "before the twins were born or had done anything good or bad — in order that God's purpose in election might stand."

panels.ctx: The oracle to Rebekah (v.23) overturns the natural birth order before the boys are born — "the older shall serve the younger." The entire Jacob story unfolds under this reversal. The birthright sale (vv.29–34) shows Jacob acquiring by cunning what God had already promised by oracle — creating a character study of someone who grasps at what God would have given anyway. The pattern will cost Jacob decades of exile.

cross.note: Paul uses the Esau/Jacob reversal as his foundational text for divine election: "Though they were not yet born and had done nothing either good or bad...she was told, 'The older will serve the younger.'" God's choice precedes human action.
Hebrews uses Esau as a warning against being "sexually immoral or unholy like Esau, who sold his birthright for a single meal" — a type of those who trade the spiritual for the immediate.
"Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated" — the Malachi reference cited by Paul in Romans 9 takes the Genesis narrative into prophetic and theological territory.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The birth narrative of Jacob and Esau contains the three elements of a birth oracle: the divine consultation (Rebekah inquires of the LORD), the oracle (two nations in your womb; the older will serve the younger), and the fulfilment (the twins born in characteristic postures — Esau red and hairy, Jacob grasping Esau's heel). Sarna: "The oracle of the elder serving the younger reverses the expected social order — it is the divine right of primogeniture over human convention."

The birthright trade is one of the most economically compressed moral narratives in Genesis. Sarna: "Esau's dismissal of his birthright — 'what good is the birthright to me?' — is the defining characterisation of his life. He is the man who lives for the present moment ('I am about to die') at the expense of the future. The writer's summary — 'so Esau despised his birthright' — passes judgment without moralising."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rebekah's prayer and the divine oracle. Alter: "Rebekah, like Sarah before her, is initially barren. Isaac prays; she conceives. The struggle within the womb prompts her to enquire of the LORD — and receives an oracle about nations, not just individuals. The story of Jacob and Esau is simultaneously the story of Israel and Edom. The personal and the political are one."

The lentil stew exchange. Alter: "The episode is told with an almost comic speed: Esau arrives famished, demands the stew, Jacob names his price, Esau swears, Jacob gives him bread and lentil stew. 'He ate and drank, and then got up and left.' Five verbs of consumption and departure, each word a judgment. 'So Esau despised his birthright.'"

The oracle — "the older will serve the younger" — is the divine reversal of natural order. Calvin: "God here openly declares that the order of nature is to be subverted, in order to show that the election of Jacob was altogether free and gratuitous. The purpose of God is not founded on any anticipated merit but on his own will alone." Paul cites this oracle in Romans 9 as the foundation of the doctrine of election.

Esau's despising of the birthright is the paradigm of the profane person in Hebrews 12:16. Calvin: "Esau did not merely sell the birthright for a bowl of pottage; he showed by the transaction what he truly valued. He chose present satisfaction over future inheritance. This is the spirit of worldliness: the visible and immediate is preferred to the invisible and ultimate."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "the older will serve the younger" discusses the grammatical ambiguity: the Hebrew could be read "the older will serve the younger" or "the older, the younger will serve." The note concludes that the context (and Paul's reading in Romans 9:12) supports the former reading. The note also observes that the oracle sets up the entire Jacob-Esau narrative in which the pattern of reversal will be repeatedly worked out.

The NET note on "so Esau despised his birthright" observes that this is the narrator's theological evaluation — not merely a report of what happened but a judgment on Esau's character. The note connects to Hebrews 12:16, which calls Esau "godless" (bebelos — profane, secular) for this exchange.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.role: Patriarch; "full of years"; gathered to his people
ppl.text: His death is described with the most gracious language in the OT — no tragedy, no regret, a life completed. He sees some of the promise fulfilled (Isaac born, married, blessed by God) though not the nation or the full land. He dies as Hebrews 11 describes: in faith, not sight.

ppl.name: Esau
ppl.role: Firstborn son; hunter; man of the present moment
ppl.text: His identity is appetite and immediacy: "I am about to die; of what use is a birthright to me?" He values present hunger over future inheritance. The narrative does not hate Esau — he is vigorous, real, understandable — but it marks him as one who lives for today at the expense of tomorrow.

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.role: Second-born; craftsman; man of the future
ppl.text: His name means "he supplants/grasps the heel" — a portent of his character. He acquires by calculation what God had already promised by oracle. His grasping nature will require decades of divine discipline to transform him into "Israel" (he who struggles with God).

ppl.name: Rebekah
ppl.text: She alone knows the oracle — "the older shall serve the younger." Her later favoritism of Jacob (ch.27) must be read in this light, even if her methods are wrong. She is trying to fulfill what God has promised.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples from within you will be separated; one people will be stronger than the other, and the older will serve the younger."ESV"Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples from within you shall be divided; the one shall be stronger than the other, the older shall serve the younger."KJV"Two nations are in thy womb, and two manner of people shall be separated from thy bowels; and the one people shall be stronger than the other people; and the elder shall serve the younger."NASB"Two nations are in your womb; and two peoples will be separated from your body; and one people will be stronger than the other; and the older will serve the younger."

src.title: Mari Prophetic Texts
src.quote: The oracle to Rebekah during her difficult pregnancy ("She went to inquire of the LORD") parallels ANE practices of seeking oracles at sanctuaries during pregnancy. Mari texts describe women seeking divine guidance for difficult pregnancies.

rec.text: Paul's use of the Esau/Jacob election as his key text for divine sovereignty is one of the most contested passages in NT theology. He reads the prenatal election as proof that God's choice is based on "him who calls, not on works or merit." This generates the Calvinist/Arminian debate about predestination that has shaped Western Christianity.

The warning against "Esau-type" faith — selling eternal inheritance for immediate gratification — has made Esau a pastoral warning figure across church history. His regret (v.34 LXX: he was "rejected" though he "sought the blessing with tears") is a sobering portrait.

rows.label: Together at the Tomb: Division Resolved
rows.text: Isaac and Ishmael bury their father together. No dialogue, no explanation. Whatever divided them in life, Abraham's death is answered by his sons' shared act of honour.

rows.label: The Birthright Sold: Measured in a Bowl
rows.text: He ate, drank, rose, and left -- so Esau despised his birthright. The narrative's verdict is in the verb sequence. The birthright is measured in a bowl of stew and dismissed with the rising from the table.

debate.title: Romans 9 and the Jacob-Esau Election: Corporate or Individual?

positions.name: Individual election to salvation
positions.argument: Paul's use of Jacob/Esau in Rom 9:10-13 is about individual predestination to salvation. The prenatal choice establishes God's sovereign freedom in election apart from works.

positions.name: Corporate/national election
positions.proponents: Cranfield, Dunn, Wright, many NT scholars
positions.argument: Paul's primary reference is to the corporate election of Israel-over-Edom — the choice of Jacob's line as the covenant people. Election in Genesis 25 is about covenant headship, not necessarily eternal destiny.

positions.name: Historical then typological
positions.argument: The Genesis narrative is about covenant succession, not eternal salvation. Paul draws on it for a broader argument about God's sovereign freedom in the new covenant. Both the historical and theological applications are valid.
